text: The autonomic nervous system operates as two interlocking governors: the sympathetic branch accelerates the body toward action, while the parasympathetic branch — mediated largely by the vagus nerve — applies the brake. Under sustained stress, the sympathetic branch dominates, keeping the body in a low-grade state of readiness that physiologists call hyperarousal. Stephen Porges' Polyvagal Theory describes a more nuanced hierarchy: only when the ventral vagal system is active do we access the calm, socially connected state that supports deep focus. Chronic distraction is not merely a habit failure — it is often a sign that the nervous system never fully disengaged from its threat-scanning posture.
question: According to Polyvagal Theory, what condition is necessary for deep focus?

options.text: Dominant activity in the sympathetic nervous system
Suppression of all vagal nerve activity
Activation of the dorsal vagal shutdown response
Active engagement of the ventral vagal system
